example_prompt: Handle Bictorys payment webhook notifications to automatically confirm orders when a payment succeeds.

input_schema.description: Payload Bictorys POSTs to your webhook URL when a payment event occurs.

id.description: Unique transaction identifier. Pass to verify_transaction for server-side confirmation.

amount.description: Amount paid by the customer in the stated currency.

settledAmount.description: Amount received after currency conversion, in settledCurrency.

paymentReference.description: Your paymentReference from create_charge. Use this to match the webhook to your order.

merchantReference.description: Your merchantReference from create_charge, if provided.

orderType.description: Origin of the charge: invoice, paymentlink, flat, order.

orderId.description: Associated invoice, order, or payment link identifier.

orderDetails.description: Line items if the charge included an itemized order.

status.description: Payment outcome. Note: uppercase in webhook payloads (vs lowercase in verify_transaction responses).

response_schema.description: Your endpoint must return HTTP 200. The response body is ignored by Bictorys.

gotchas: Validate the X-Secret-Key header on every incoming webhook request: compare it with your BICTORYS_WEBHOOK_SECRET (from Bictorys dashboard → Developers → API Keys & Webhooks). Reject any request where the header is missing or does not match. Bictorys does not use HMAC — it sends the raw secret.
Return HTTP 200 immediately before performing any async work (database writes, calls to verify_transaction). Bictorys retries on non-200 responses, and a slow handler risks duplicate processing.
Never fulfill an order based on the webhook payload status alone. Always call verify_transaction server-side with the payload's id field to confirm the payment before marking an order as paid.
The status field in the webhook payload is UPPERCASE (SUCCEEDED, FAILED) whereas verify_transaction returns lowercase (succeeded, failed). Do not compare these directly without normalizing case.
Implement idempotency using the transaction id field. Bictorys may deliver the same webhook event more than once; processing the same id twice must not double-fulfill an order.
Your webhook URL must be publicly reachable over HTTPS. During local development, use a tunnel (e.g. ngrok) and update the webhook URL in your Bictorys dashboard.
